nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs; commonly found in large aggregations around coral formations, caves, and wrecks during the day. Juveniles tend to occupy seagrass beds near patch reefs.

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming fish, shrimp, crabs, stomatopods, cephalopods, and planktonic crustaceans, along with some plant and algal material. In captivity, has been reported to accept chopped frozen silversides, krill, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and other carnivore preparations. Generally advised to offer varied meaty foods to maintain condition.

goodWith: other large predators of similar size
avoidWith: invertebrates small enough to be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: Uxbona; 3072x2304. Large vibrant school of Lutjanus kasmira over reef, sharp, true yellow bodies with blue horizontal stripes clearly visible; best represents this shoaling species.
Rickard Zerpe; 2533x1900. Tack-sharp full-body lateral profile of a single vivid individual; four blue stripes and yellow body diagnostic and crisp.
Rickard Zerpe; 2043x1532. Sharp close-up of a vibrant individual (with second fish behind); clear yellow-and-blue striping. Slightly tighter crop than the recommended pick.

summary: A boldly striped yellow-and-blue schooling snapper from the Indo-Pacific, generally considered suitable for large fish-only systems with live rock. It tends to be semi-aggressive and will readily consume small fish and invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Bluestripe Snapper (Lutjanus kasmira) is a striking Indo-Pacific species with a bright yellow body crossed by four vivid blue horizontal stripes. It is widely distributed from the Red Sea and East Africa to Hawaii, Marquesas, and Line Islands, ranging north to southern Japan and south to Australia.

Due to its active schooling nature and substantial bioload when kept in a group, hobbyists are generally advised to provide at least around 1,700 liters, and leaning toward more space is recommended wherever possible.

Temperament sources vary, but this species tends to be an aggressive feeder that can intimidate smaller or more passive tankmates. It is generally recommended to introduce it last into an established community to reduce the likelihood of it dominating other fish.

In the wild, Bluestripe Snappers are frequently observed in large aggregations. This schooling behavior carries into captivity, and groups are considered viable in sufficiently large systems.

Reef compatibility is generally regarded as requiring caution: this species tends to eat small ornamental shrimp, crabs, and other small crustaceans, as well as small fish, and is generally considered unsuitable for reef systems housing such invertebrates.

Lifespan has been reported at up to six years. This species produces a significant bioload, so robust filtration and regular water changes are generally advised. It tends to grow quickly when well-fed and is broadly regarded as hardy once established, though the large tank size requirement and semi-aggressive temperament make it an intermediate-level species rather than a straightforward beginner choice.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for this species in home aquariums. Wild individuals are reported to reach sexual maturity at approximately 20.5 cm. No captive-bred offspring are currently available in the trade.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (40 cm max, 25 cm common), temperature range (19.1–28.3°C), native range, diet, taxonomy, and lifespan (max 6 years). FishBase max size of 40 cm differs from LiveAquaria's 25.4 cm (10 in); averaged across all sources to arrive at ~36 cm maximum.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (40 cm), minimum tank volume (2000 L), salinity (~1.026 SG), temperament (extremely aggressive — disagreeing with LiveAquaria's 'peaceful'; semi-aggressive used as average), and reef compatibility (with caution).

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (35 cm), minimum tank volume (2000 L), temperature range (19.1–28.3°C). Adult size of 35 cm and tank minimum of 2000 L are consistent with FishBase and ReefApp, supporting the larger end of the range estimates.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Wild individuals are reported to reach sexual maturity at approximately 20.5 cm.
